the mcp server -- how does it work? when the mcp server runs `who` tool what do we show it if it's been started without the context of an agent?

with no agent context it should create an agent context for that session, ideally showing it as "chatgpt/echo123" for chatgpt or "grok/echo123" for grok -- I think we can associate, at least for chatgpt, based on session-id x-openai header or something like that

Good signal: the MCP `initialize` handshake already receives `clientInfo` (name/version) from the connecting client and even logs it (`src/cli/mcp/access_log.rs:31-32`) — but it's discarded, not threaded through to session resolution. That's a more reliable identity source than sniffing HTTP headers, since header-sniffing (`x-openai-...`) only works in `--http` mode; the default stdio transport (which is how most harnesses spawn this) has no headers at all, only inherited env vars.

My recommendation: capture `clientInfo.name` at `initialize` time, stash it against the connection/session in the daemon, and have `rpc_who`'s no-anchor fallback auto-provision a session (`<clientInfo.name>/<random-suffix>`, matching the existing `agent/session-fragment` convention already visible in the fabric — e.g. `@codex/te-18c0e7f875d4bb90-3`) instead of collapsing to an anonymous empty-string snapshot.

Main tradeoff: this turns `who` from a pure read into something with a side effect (creates a session record on first call), and `clientInfo.name` is self-reported by the client so it's identity-by-assertion, not verified — fine for display/awareness purposes, not for anything security-sensitive. Also need to decide persistence: keyed per MCP connection (dies with the process) vs. something stabler if the same external session reconnects.

Want me to look at how sessions get created/registered elsewhere in the daemon (to see if there's already a "create session" path `rpc_who` could call into), before sketching an actual plan?
not rpc_who; that's too narrow -- it should be set to whatever sets the agent identity, not just `who`

Clean — compiles, clippy-clean, and formatted correctly. Confirmed the file-state warnings above were stale echoes, not an actual revert (Read confirmed unchanged since my edits).

## Summary

Threaded `clientInfo.name` through the MCP **HTTP** transport (stdio deferred per your earlier call):

- **`src/cli/mcp/http.rs`**: added `Mcp-Session-Id` tracking. On `initialize`, mints a session id (`mcp-<nanos>-<counter>`, matching the existing `mint_session_id()` convention), captures `clientInfo.name` from the request, stores it in a new `HttpState.mcp_sessions` map, and returns the id via the `Mcp-Session-Id` response header (also exposed/allowed via CORS). On every later request, reads the incoming `Mcp-Session-Id` header and looks up the stored name.
- **`src/cli/mcp/tools.rs`**: `call()` and every RPC-building function (`who`, `chat_read`, `chat_write`, `channels_create`, `channel_mutation`, `daemon_identity`) now take `client_info_name: Option<&str>` and merge it into the outgoing daemon params as `"client_info_name"` via a new `with_client_info` helper — sitting alongside the existing `pty_session`/`harness`/`agent` envelope fields.
- **`src/cli/mcp.rs`**: stdio call site passes `None` (unchanged behavior there, as agreed).

Not yet done (separate follow-up): nothing on the daemon side consumes `client_info_name` yet — `CallerAnchor`/`resolve_session_inner` still ignore it. This change only gets the value onto the wire; the auto-provisioning ("no session → synthesize `chatgpt/echo123`") logic we discussed earlier still needs to be built on top of it. Also worth flagging: the `mcp_sessions` map has no eviction, so a long-lived tunnel accumulates one entry per distinct MCP client connection.
